data.title: Peloton: Quarterly Recap And Current Musings
data.description:
In early February, Peloton Interactive, Inc. (PTON) reported Q2 earnings, and this stock has slid since.

Q2 Recap

In the quarter, PTON reported top-line revenue of $657M, a decrease of $17M, or 3% y/y. Gross margin improved in the quarter, landing at 50.5%, an increase of 3.20% y/y. Adjusted EBITDA was $81M, coming in 23% higher on a year-over-year basis. GAAP net loss was $39M, and free cash flow was $72M.

Additionally, in the quarter, Peloton announced that (i) its net debt decreased 52% y/y, and (ii) their integrated commercial business unit introduced the Peloton Pro Series (Peloton commercial equipment), and this business unit achieved double-digit y/y growth.

The most negative from the quarter was that ending paid connected fitness subscriptions continued to fall--with 2.66M reported (a decrease of 214K, or 7%, on a y/y basis). I think that continued fall of members was the catalyst for the sell-off post-earnings release. Wall Street now views this as a cigar butt business that is shrinking over time.

Peloton gave relatively upbeat guidance for FY 2026, pasted below, but despite that, Mr. Market is not believing this story, and the stock is trading at a very depressed level.

Full Year FY26 Outlook

  • Total Revenue outlook of $2.40 billion to $2.44 billion, representing a decrease of $71 million, or 3%, year-over-year at the midpoint and a decrease to our outlook provided last quarter.
  • Total Gross Margin outlook of approximately 53.0%, reflecting an increase of 210 bps year-over-year and a 100 bps increase to our outlook provided last quarter.
  • Adjusted EBITDA outlook of $450 million to $500 million, representing an increase of $71 million, or 18%, year-over-year at the midpoint and an increase of $25 million to our outlook provided last quarter.
  • Free Cash Flow minimum target of $275 million, an increase of $25 million from our minimum target provided last quarter.

Churn in the last quarter was up more than historical figures, likely because of the increase in the subscription cost. In October, there were a number of price increases, most notably, the all-access membership increased from $44 to $49.99. There was also a slew of new upgrades like AI personalized plans to provide more value, but a number of members decided to cancel following the price increase. Further, as I'll discuss below in more detail, Peloton has been prudently cutting costs, including trimming marketing costs so, to me, this decline makes sense and should have been expected given the inputs.

What I love to see is the Adjusted EBITDA outlook of $450M to $500M with a free cash flow minimum target of $275M. A business that is de-levering and generating significant free cash flow should not be trading like it is going to go out of business.

Valuation

At the current trading level, Peloton sits at just 4.525x its free cash flow. This, in my view, is an absurdly low level for Peloton's business and potential positive catalysts on the horizon.

By way of example, Planet Fitness (PLNT) currently trades at 26x its price-to- free cash flow and I would argue that Peloton's current business model is less capital intensive than PLNT's.

To give you a sense of what some other companies with mature and profitable subscription businesses trade at, Netflix trades at 44.95x, Spotify at 34.23x, and Roku at 46.37x. Two things to call out with these comparisons: (1) each of those businesses is surely operating much better than Peloton currently is, and (2) those are their depressed trading ranges, as those stocks have sold off a bit this year.

Outlook and Opportunities

Given the business is free cash flow positive and they are quickly paying down debt, I am comfortable with Peloton in my portfolio. Management had lost the right to play offense for a while and prudently cut costs and retreated from seeking growth opportunities. You can see this by looking at their total operating expenses, which came in at $588M for the six months ended December 31, 2025 (down from $655M in the prior year over the same period). At the same time, their interest expense is falling, and it looks like they are beginning the expansion of new verticals to drive the top-line once again.

In a prior article, back in December, I noted a few potential growth avenues, which I won't detail here. However, I will note a few others and provide an update on one of the opportunities.

In a Peloton Sub-Reddit, some users noted that Peloton was sending out surveys about vitamins.

Peloton is sending out applications for participation in small study groups (and is providing a $125 gift card for those ultimately selected and participating). Based on the intake form, the questions were primarily focused on vitamins and supplements. Peloton is likely doing market research on creating their own or partnering with a vitamin producer to create their own line of supplement products.

As detailed back in December, this can be a great way to capitalize on its brand value; they have many loyal fans that would be excited about this new vertical. The supplement business generally has high gross margins, and even a distressed FBA supplement brand that I found on QuietLight.com reportedly has 80%+ gross margins. This would, in my view, be a very prudent new vertical to add. I just hope that Peloton finds a great partner to do it with, as opposed to pursuing M&A or buying their own factory.

In addition, there is still low-hanging fruit that Peloton can capture, such as giving users the ability to pay via ACH, as opposed to credit cards, which should cut down on interchange fees and help reduce the number of chargebacks. This tweak won't make a huge difference but should incrementally help. Peloton was in fight-or-flight mode before, focused on stemming the losses, but now has the breathing room to make these sorts of changes and help optimize the business.

During the earnings call, there was a short but important point that was emphasized by management that I also think will be impactful moving forward:

As we discussed last quarter, in Q1 of fiscal 2026, we began assigning executive compensation and other corporate overhead expenses associated with our corporate facilities across the P&L, as we focus on driving more accountability for costs at a functional level. Prior to fiscal 2026, these costs were all recorded in G&A but are now assigned to cost of goods sold, sales and marketing, G&A and R&D. All of the year-over-year changes discussed today are referencing last year on an as-reported basis.

When you have a business that has all costs allocated to a general pool, there is no accountability; no one really cares about the costs, and it is hard to optimize the business and know what is really important. Switching to a method of assigning costs across the P&L will really help to uncover opportunities. I would love to see individual segment leads have a component of their compensation tied to how their division performs and hope that is something the Board asks for soon.

Another growth avenue that Peloton is in a beta launch for currently is incorporating personal training into the platform with the launch of Peloton x Trainwell. This gives you a personal trainer for $99.99 per month and is only available for active All-Access Members.

Source: Peloton's Website

Partnering with Trainwell to launch this was a smart move. Peloton did not need to hire trainers or build out much to launch this, and these sorts of low-downside, high-upside opportunities are what I love to see from companies. I hope we get some details at some point about what the economics look like for this offering, i.e., what the split of the monthly fee between Peloton and Trainwell is. There was a review from someone that is in the Beta, and it was very positive. Other users on Facebook have commented that they're enjoying it too--which is good anecdotal evidence. It's hard to extrapolate from such a small sample, but nonetheless, it's still a positive, especially since this could make staying on the platform stickier for members.

Risks

Given where the stock is trading and how the business is performing, I don't see much more downside from here. In any event, the biggest risk to this business, in my view, is that growth never returns and it stays in a bounded range for a while. Another risk, which I think is unlikely, is that management begins to pursue M&A again and attempts to expand inorganically. They have not yet won the right to pursue M&A again, so I hope they focus on these asymmetric opportunities, i.e., small downside, large upside.

Conclusion

At this trading level, I think an investment in Peloton presents a great opportunity for mitigated downside but huge upside.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: Geopolitics Overpower Fundamentals - The $150 Oil Warning And The Rate Cut Dilemma
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Wall Street’s primary indexes tumbled on Friday, led by a sharp decline in the Dow to a three-month low as the market grappled with escalating conflict in the Middle East and a surprising contraction in the US labor market.

Data revealed the economy unexpectedly lost jobs in February, exacerbated by healthcare strikes and severe winter weather pushing the unemployment rate up to 4.4%.

This combination of geopolitical tension and economic cooling has shifted market expectations; traders have now priced in a roughly 50% chance of a June interest rate cut, while some analysts suggest the Fed’s dual mandate to balance inflation and employment could pull the first cut forward to July.

The volatility is being fueled by a dramatic spike in energy prices, with Brent crude hitting $90 a barrel following disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. As shipping halts and analysts warn that oil could reach $150 a barrel if Gulf exports are fully suspended, airline stocks have plummeted nearly 13% this week.

Qatar’s recent warnings regarding prolonged delivery delays for natural gas have only added to the "stagflation" fears, a situation where growth slows while prices rise.

Despite the downturn, US markets have shown more resilience than their global counterparts, buoyed by a strong tech sector and the nation's status as a net oil exporter.

In contrast, European markets suffered their worst week in nearly a year, with the STOXX 600 hitting a two-month low. Because Europe is more vulnerable to energy price shocks, major exchanges in Frankfurt, Paris, and Madrid recorded historic weekly losses as investors braced for a potential stagflationary environment across the continent.

Gold prices edged higher on Friday as escalating tensions in the Middle East sparked a wave of safe-haven buying. Spot gold rose 0.3% to $5,090.16 per ounce, while US gold futures for April delivery climbed 0.4% to $5,099.50. Despite these daily gains, the metal remained on track for a 3.5% weekly decline, effectively snapping a four-week winning streak.

This downward pressure stemmed from persistent inflation worries and a volatile dollar, both of which have dampened investor expectations for imminent interest rate cuts.

The broader commodities market showed a stark contrast, as crude oil prices surged toward their most significant weekly gain since the 2022 invasion of Ukraine. Spot WTI oil was up around 34% at the time of writing.

While gold benefited slightly from its status as a refuge during geopolitical instability, the reality of "higher-for-longer" interest rates continues to weigh on bullion's appeal compared to yield-bearing assets.

The Week Ahead

Global markets enter the second week of March 2026 under the shadow of a rapidly escalating Middle East conflict. With a US-led campaign against Iran entering its second week and shipping through the Strait of Hormuz at a standstill, the "2022 Energy Shock" is no longer just a historical reference; it is the primary lens through which investors are viewing the week ahead.

The Macro Theme: Geopolitics Overpowers Fundamentals

While the economic calendar is packed with heavy hitters like US CPI and UK GDP, their influence may be dampened by the "high-risk zone" of current geopolitics.

  • Energy Prices as the Barometer: Brent crude has already surged toward $85/bbl. Analysts warn that a breach of $100/bbl would be a "psychological milestone" that could trigger a deeper sell-off in risk assets.
  • 2022 vs. 2026: There does appear to be a critical difference from the 2022 shock: the labor market is now much cooler. Unlike 2022, when workers could chase higher pay to offset energy costs, the current cooling trend (highlighted by a weak February US jobs report) means consumers have less of a buffer.

United States: The Inflation-Rate Cut Tug-of-War

The spotlight is firmly on the US Consumer Price Index (CPI) due Wednesday and Core PCE on Friday.

The Dilemma: Markets are looking for signs of how "sticky" inflation remains before the full impact of the current energy spike is even recorded. A surprise upside in CPI would likely force markets to price out the two Fed rate cuts currently expected for 2026.

Consumer Sentiment: Friday’s University of Michigan survey will be the first real-time look at how the "energy shock" and tariff fears are sapping household confidence.

Asia: China’s "Two Sessions" and Japan’s GDP Revision

Asia remains at the forefront of the supply chain disruption, with a specific focus on the closing of China’s National People’s Congress.

  • China (Monday/Tuesday): February CPI and PPI data will be released. Analysts expect a bounce in CPI to ~1.0% due to Lunar New Year spending, though this may be viewed as a "noise" rather than a trend. Trade data (Tuesday) will be scrutinized for the resilience of external demand.
  • Japan (Tuesday): Expect a significant upward revision to Q4 2025 GDP (from 0.1% to 0.3% QoQ) following strong capital spending and winter bonuses. This could keep the Bank of Japan on its path toward normalization despite global turmoil.

Europe & UK: Looking for Signs of Life

The Eurozone is navigating a "stagflationary shock" where energy deficits punish the euro, though narrowing interest rate differentials against the USD are providing some support.

  • Germany (Monday/Wednesday): Industrial production and final inflation data will show if the "fledgling recovery" in manufacturing can withstand the new energy spike.

United Kingdom (Friday): Monthly GDP and industrial output for January will be released. Markets are looking for a pickup in growth to confirm the encouraging signals seen in recent PMI surveys.

Chart of the week - WTI Oil

From a technical standpoint, WTI has just posted a massive bullish engulfing candle, which has completely invalidated the previous multi-year downtrend, thrusting WTI from the mid-$60s to over $90.00 in a matter of days. The move, as is largely the case, was driven by the geopolitical and fundamental dynamics around oil prices. However, it is important to note that there were technical signs that oil was in a consolidation phase with a breakout growing ever-more likely.

Nobody, however, envisioned a 30%+ price spike in the space of a week. Where does price go from here? This will, of course, depend on the course the war in the Middle East takes.

Further refinery attacks by Iran or any escalation on that front, and we could open the new week already above the $100/barrel mark. Alternatively, if tensions do begin to ease, oil prices may fall quite quickly.

The price is currently testing the $90.05 level. If the momentum continues, the next major psychological and technical targets are $93.96 and the multi-year high at $100.00. In the event of a "cool-off," the previous resistance at $80.19 and the 200-day SMA ($75.41) now serve as the primary floor.

There is a significant liquidity gap between $70 and $85. In normal market conditions, these gaps tend to fill, but in "war-premium" markets, they can remain open for weeks.

WTI Oil Weekly Chart, March 6, 2026

Original Post

data.title: SPHQ: Invesco's S&P 500 Quality ETF Is Well-Positioned To Outperform
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the Invesco S&P 500 Quality ETF (SPHQ) on November 25, 2024, when I reiterated my "buy" rating and highlighted how well it complements the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Since that review was published, SPHQ has slightly outperformed SPY with its 15.88% total return and beat the $50B iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF (QUAL) by nearly 5%.

SPHQ also easily beat the JPMorgan U.S. Quality Factor ETF (JQUA), whose fundamentals I have watched deteriorate over the last year. Therefore, I'd like to use this update to communicate to readers my belief that SPHQ is superior to JQUA and QUAL right now. Even though it's a bit expensive with its 28.05x trailing P/E, its components' 38.50% ROE is unmatched, and despite not screening for it, SPHQ owns stocks with double-digit historical and estimated EPS growth rates, making it a pretty solid play all around. I hope you enjoy this review where I reiterate my "buy" rating on SPHQ, and as always, I look forward to the discussion afterwards.

SPHQ Overview

SPHQ tracks the S&P 500 Quality Index, whose methodology document describes the selection and weighting process as follows:

1. The selection universe is the S&P 500 Index, which is comprised of 500 of the largest U.S. stocks subject to committee selection.

2. The Index calculates quality scores for each stock based on three factors: return on equity, accruals ratio, and financial leverage ratio.

3. ROE is a company's trailing twelve-month earnings per share divided by its most recent book value per share.

4. The accruals ratio is the change of a company's net operating assets over the last fiscal year divided by its average total assets over the previous two fiscal years.

5. Financial leverage is the company's latest total debt divided by its book value.

6. The Index selects 100 stocks based on quality score, with security weights determined by the product of their market cap and quality score. Security weights are constrained to the lower of 5% and 20x its market cap, while sector weights are limited to 40% based on the GICS system.

7. Lastly, the Index reconstitutes semi-annually after the third Friday in June and December. The Index selects 100 stocks (top quintile), but a 20% buffer rule allows stocks that rank in the top 120 by quality score to remain.

SPHQ's net expense ratio is 0.15% (0.21% before waivers) and the ETF has $16.29 billion in assets under management as of March 6, 2026. The Invesco website also notes a 0.01% 30-day median bid/ask spread, 207.38M shares outstanding, and an inception date of December 6, 2005.

Needless to say, it's very well-established, though I should point out that SPHQ's tracked Index changed on March 18, 2016. Previously, it tracked the S&P 500 High Quality Rankings Index, and before June 30, 2010, it tracked the Value Line Timeliness Select Index. Therefore, I'd be careful about using performance since its inception to form an opinion about SPHQ. Invesco ETFs often change strategies, so as a practice, I advise investors to always read the footnotes included in the performance section. If the Index is missing a performance since inception figure, that's a telltale sign that the ETF's full history may not be relevant.

S&P's Quality Definition: Digging Deeper

As discussed, SPHQ uses three ratios to determine a stock's quality. It's a pretty straightforward approach, but it's actually a little bit more complex than you might think. For example, consider ROE, which, according to the DuPont formula, is the product of three ratios:

  • net margins (net income/sales)
  • asset turnover (sales/average assets)
  • financial leverage (average assets/average equity)

If you're familiar with cross multiplication, you'll notice the numerators and denominators for sales and average assets cross each out other, leaving us only with net income / average equity, which is ROE. Critics of this capital efficiency ratio will correctly argue that a company can boost its ROE by simply increasing its financial leverage, but remember that SPHQ's Index accounts for this by also ranking stocks by financial leverage, albeit in a modified way (total debt divided by book value). In a nutshell, the aim is to hold companies that are using its assets efficiently to generate sales (asset turnover) and profits (net margins) without the use of excessive leverage, which I find reasonable.

That said, the reality is that stocks in certain sectors like Technology have a structural advantage over others because they tend to be light on assets and have strong pricing power. Maybe that won't change your opinion about S&P's ranking system, but to demonstrate the impact, please consider the following ratios for each of the 11 S&P 500 equal-weight sector ETFs and the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

As shown, Consumer Discretionary (RSPD) has the highest ROE of 28.42%, but S&P's quality system would take points off for its high average leverage, as measured by its 2.28x debt/equity ratio. In contrast, Industrials (RSPN) and Technology (RSPT) look pretty solid, as their net margins, asset turnover, and debt/equity ratios are reasonably close to or better than RSP's, while sectors like Utilities (RSPU) don't really have a chance at getting good representation in SPHQ. ROE really isn't a good indicator of quality in this largely-regulated sector, as asset bases and debt use are typically high.

With these structural differences in mind, I think you'll find SPHQ's current sector composition makes sense. As of February 28, 2026, Technology and Industrials comprised more than 50% of the fund, followed by Consumer Staples (16%) and Financials (14%). These four sectors rank #3, #2, #4, and #5 by ROE in the table above, while low-ROE sectors like Utilities and Materials have weak representation.

My Take On Quality

I don't claim to have a universal definition for quality, but when I update my rankings for ETFs each week, quality is a key focus for me, and it's the factor I overweight the most. Here's my current breakdown of the components that feed into my system's quality score:

  • Return on Total Capital: 20%
  • Net Margin: 20%
  • Asset Turnover: 12.5%
  • EBITDA Margins: 10%
  • EBIT Margins: 10%
  • Return on Assets: 10%
  • Return on Equity: 5%
  • Gross Margin: 2.5%
  • Current Ratio: 2.5%
  • Quick Ratio: 2.5%
  • Sector Adjustment: 20%

As shown, I place the most emphasis on return on capital (operating profit divided by debt + assets) to improve cross-sector comparisons, and I also take the step of adjusting the score based on the ETF's sector composition. Leverage is missing, as that feeds into my risk score instead, but overall, it's fairly comprehensive, and I'm comforted that under this system, SPHQ scores quite well with a 9.02/10 quality score.

Technically, QUAL has a higher quality score at 9.62/10, which is deserved, given its higher margin and capital efficiency ratios. However, SPHQ's score, by definition, puts it in the top 10% of the 1000+ U.S. Equity ETFs I track. That's probably sufficient enough, and as I'll demonstrate shortly, it does a little better than QUAL on other key factors like risk and size.

SPHQ Performance Analysis

First though, let's review SPHQ's performance, which I've limited to the period between December 1, 2017, and March 5, 2026, to account for JQUA's inception date. As shown below, its 13.78% annualized return was essentially identical to SPY's 13.77%, and it has a slight edge on risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios). SPHQ also had similar volatility (15.50% vs. 16.15% annualized standard deviation), maximum drawdown (-24.33% vs. -23.93%), and worst 12 months (-16.79% vs. 18.17%) as SPY.

SPHQ has an edge over QUAL on total returns, risk-adjusted returns, and drawdowns, while JQUA, at least over this full period, looks more competitive. The table reveals a maximum drawdown that was more than 3% better than SPHQ's, plus better downside risk-adjusted returns (1.16 Sortino ratio). From the calendar-year returns table below, you'll notice that JQUA did well in the two down market years (2018 and 2022), so while I'm disappointed in its deteriorating fundamentals (including its surprisingly weak 7.65/10 quality score noted earlier), I'm still counting on it to perform well over a full cycle.

Lastly, I want to touch on recovery times after drawdowns, because, unless you're an impatient investor who overreacts to big losses, the total time from peak to trough is what matters. Fortunately, if you're an SPHQ shareholder, the data is in your favor. Based on the tables below, we see that SPHQ declined by 24.33% from January 2022 to September 2022 but was fully recovered by July 2023, meaning investors were underwater for 19 months. That's the same number of months as JQUA, despite its drawdown depth being greater (24.33% vs. 22.15%).

SPHQ's 19-month peak-to-trough time was also five months shorter than QUAL's and SPY's, so by this measure, it's navigated market cycles quite well.

SPHQ Analysis

Sector Allocations and Overlap Analysis

SPHQ's sector allocations are subject to change at each reconstitution, but you have a better chance of getting something "different" compared to QUAL because the latter tracks an Index that aims to be sector-neutral with the broader market. That's why QUAL and SPY have similar sector compositions, whereas SPHQ is the odd one out in this group. As shown below, it currently overweights Industrials and Consumer Staples by 15.03% and 10.64%, respectively, while underweighting Communication Services (-8.22%) and Technology (-6.24%).

In terms of fit, SPHQ also overlaps with SPY the least of the three - only 19.28% as of February 27, 2026, compared to 45.06% and 40.42% for QUAL and JQUA, respectively. The top common holdings table highlights how it accomplishes this mainly by having 0% allocated to NVidia Corporation (NVDA), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Alphabet Inc.(GOOGL), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), so overall, I view SPHQ as the best complement to SPY right now in terms of both sector composition and individual holdings.

SPHQ Fundamental Analysis: Top 20 Holdings

Next, I've compiled a list of fundamental statistics for SPHQ's top 20 holdings, which comprise 62.87% of the portfolio. My goal is to demonstrate that SPHQ is attractive beyond its strong quality features highlighted earlier. Please have a scan of the following table, which covers the size, risk, growth, sentiment, value, quality, and momentum factors, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

1. SPHQ has the lowest weighted average market capitalization ($397 billion) of the four, suggesting that it's equipped to perform well if mega-cap stocks fall out of favor. I think most investors, including myself, already have enough mega-cap exposure through Index funds like SPY (and even more if you hold growth-oriented funds like QQQ), so SPHQ is a way to knock that exposure down a bit to more reasonable levels. As a bonus, moving away from the market's most volatile names like Nvidia reduces portfolio beta. As shown above, SPHQ's five-year portfolio beta of 1.03 (based on current holdings) is below SPY's 1.13, and since we know that SPHQ complements SPY at the sector and company levels, the data suggests the two held in tandem should reduce risk.

2. SPHQ trades at 28.05x trailing earnings and 24.36x forward earnings, both of which are in line with QUAL but slightly more expensive than JQUA and SPY. Its valuation is probably its weakest feature, but it's at least supported by solid earnings growth rates, especially over the last year. At the portfolio level after controlling for outliers (capped at 50% per security), SPHQ's holdings have increased their EPS by 17.43% over the last year, which has translated into a 45.45% one-year price return, the best in the group. Furthermore, SPHQ's top stocks are still trending well on earnings surprises. GE's 9.81% surprise was its 13th-consecutive quarterly beat, and at the portfolio level, SPHQ's 6.69% EPS surprise is the strongest of the four. For those wondering, I use EPS surprise trends to gauge market sentiment, and it's always reassuring when I see stocks performing well for reasons backed by real results rather than speculation alone.

3. From an estimated growth-at-a-reasonable-price perspective, JQUA is technically the best of the three quality funds. To illustrate, consider these modified forward PEG ratios using each ETF's one-year estimated EPS growth rate and their forward P/E ratios:

  • SPHQ: 24.36x / 14.49% = 1.68x
  • QUAL: 24.20x / 15.77% = 1.53x
  • JQUA: 21.11x / 14.85% = 1.42x

However, JQUA doesn't fare nearly as well on a trailing basis (3Y EPS CAGR and TTM P/E), which might indicate an earnings durability problem. Put differently, since its components' estimated earnings growth rates are well above their historical averages, they might be more prone to a valuation re-rating if those growth rates don't materialize.

  • SPHQ: 28.05x / 11.68% = 2.40x
  • QUAL: 28.05x / 15.57% =1.80x
  • JQUA: 26.08x / 9.95% = 2.62x

To be sure, SPHQ has the same "issue", just to a lesser extent, whereas QUAL's growth rates are remarkably consistent. I actually think QUAL is well-positioned right now, but I'm still concerned by its higher 1.11 portfolio beta, high concentration in mega-caps, and slower recovery time after the 2022 market downturn.

Investment Recommendation

SPHQ's Index might define "quality" in a somewhat simplistic way, but after digging deeper and calculating additional profitability and capital efficiency ratios at the portfolio level, I found that it currently ranks among the top 10% of all 1000+ U.S. Equity ETFs I track. SPHQ also holds stocks with high historical and estimated EPS growth rates and above-average earnings surprises, so I'm confident the success its components have had over the last year is well-deserved. If I had to pick one weakness, I'd say it's valuation - SPHQ's 28.05x trailing P/E is two points higher than SPY's. However, its 1.03 portfolio beta is lower, it's not overly concentrated in mega-cap growth, and the ETF itself has a good history of recovering quickly after drawdowns. Add in a reasonably low 0.15% expense ratio and strong liquidity, and I'm confident in reiterating my "buy" rating from November 2024.

I hope you found this analysis helpful, and as always, I hope you'll reach out with any questions in the comments section below. Thanks for reading.

data.title: DGRO: The Perfect Dividend ETF To Navigate The Storm
data.description:
Overview

Market indices continue to trade sideways with heightened volatility related to the macro enviroment, global conflict, and uncertainty related to the technology sector. Through these challenges, the iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) continues to demonstrate why it is one of the highest quality long-term dividend growth ETFs in the market. Moreover, these unique market conditions have almost created a 'perfect storm' scenario where investors get to clearly see the strength in its portfolio quality. DGRO has outperformed the market indices over the last few months, and if the start of the year is an indication to how the remainder of 2026 will go, DGRO is well positioned to continue navigating this storm.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that DGRO's share price has increased by a little more than 13%. DGRO continues to demonstrate its ability to participate in the market's positive momentum. When including all dividends that were paid out to investors, the total return jumps up to nearly 17.2% over the same time frame. DGRO's starting dividend yield is quite low at 2%, but the magic is in its ability to provide a growing stream of income over a longer holding period.

When I previously covered DGRO, I issued a buy rating and discussed why its a great ETF for investors that are approaching retirement. Since then, DGRO has provided a double-digit total return that has outpaced the S&P 500. With markets off to a bad start for 2026, I wanted to revisit DGRO to reassess its current value propositions, some of its strengths, and its outlook for the remainder of the year. I believe that DGRO remains a great option for accumulators that want a straightforward buy-and-hold ETF, which can be attributed to its unique portfolio strategy.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's latest overview, DGRO has total net assets of $38.1 billion that are allocated across nearly 400 different positions. The fund is very cost-efficient since its expense ratio sits at 0.08% of assets. DGRO's goal is to track the performance of the Morningstar U.S. Dividend Growth Index and it achieves this goal by providing exposure to U.S. companies that have an established track record of attractive dividend growth metrics. However, DGRO takes it a step further by filtering down its holdings to the companies that have met the following criteria:

  1. Increased dividends for at least five consecutive years.
  2. A positive earnings outlook.
  3. Payout ratio must be below 75%.

Expanding on this structure, DGRO maintains an efficient level of diversity across sectors. As we can see below, DGRO is most heavily allocated to financials at 18.65% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to health care and technology companies, which accounts for 17.4% and 14.82% of assets respectively. The ETF will rebalance its holdings every single quarter, so these allocations will shift periodically.

Additionally, DGRO reconstitutes its positioning once per year in December. This ensures that the fund always has exposure to companies that meet its criteria, which results in dropping underperforming names, while adding in new positions. This is why the top holdings may look a bit different from the time of my last coverage. For instance, Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) is now the largest individual holding at 3.6% of assets. DGRO also provides exposure to many other high quality dividend growth companies, including Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), AbbVie, Inc. (ABBV), JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Apple Inc. (AAPL), and The Procter & Gamble Company (PG), just to name a few.

However, I wanted to specifically highlight DGRO's allocation to technology companies. As you may already know, the technology sector isn't known for its ability to provide a large amount of companies that have a history of prioritizing dividend growth. Instead, many of these technology companies choose to reinvest their earnings back into the growth of their business. However, DGRO is able to identify some of the highest quality dividend growth companies in the sector, making it unique amongst peers because this allows DGRO to directly participate in the growth of the technology sector as AI becomes a large driver of growth. For instance, DGRO provides direct exposure to companies like:

  • Microsoft Corporation (MSFT)
  • Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO)
  • Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN)
  • Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO)
  • Oracle Corporation (ORCL)
  • QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM)

With AI being such a disruptor in the market, it is estimated that technology will continue to be a driver of growth for the indices. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase to ~$3.5 trillion by 2033. This would represent a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% through 2033. Even if this outlook is off by 50%, this would still represent a massive double-digit growth potential and DGRO is directly aligned to partially participate in this because of the technology exposure.

Performance Insights

I believe that the exposure to technology allows DGRO to participate in rapid upside growth of the markets. However, the allocation to many other areas of the market also means that the ETF can remain more resilient during market downturns. Since the start of 2026, we've seen the SaaS companies sell off from their highs. AI has become so good that investors believe it may threaten the moat to companies that rely on recurring subscription revenue from software. Thankfully, DGRO's allocation to more traditional dividend-focused areas of the market, like consumer defensive, industrial, and energy companies, can help offset these declines.

These other areas of the markets tend to be less vulnerable to the threat of AI due to their more tangible business models and cyclical strengths. This has resulted in DGRO holding up a lot better than the market indices. For instance, the State Street S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) have traded sideways and slightly down over the last three months. However, DGRO has been able to remain resilient and outperformed these traditional index-tracking ETFs. DGRO's total return remains at 4.4% over the last three months, which continues to outpace SPY and QQQ.

Forward looking, DGRO's performance may continue in the near-term if capital flows out of technology and into other areas of the market. The rotation out of the fast growing technology companies can cause investors to seek more value-oriented positions, which could boost sectors that have underperformed over the last few quarters. For instance, the threat of tariffs has suppressed earnings in the consumer staples sectors, while higher interest rates have suppressed some utilities. A rotation back into these underserved areas of the market can cause DGRO to continue its outperformance.

Even if the market does decline further, I believe that DGRO will hold up a lot better than these index-tracking ETFs due to its better diversity. A good example of this was demonstrated through the market decline of 2022. SPY saw a total loss of 18.17% and QQQ experienced a large loss of 32.58%. Although DGRO also declined, it was able to preserve investor capital a lot better with a loss of 7.9% over the same period.

However, this doesn't necessarily mean that DGRO will always outperform the indices. In fact, the focus on dividend growth has historically hurt returns over a longer holding period. By excluding companies that do not pay dividends, DGRO is not able to provide exposure to high quality businesses that continue to grow earnings and free cash flows. For instance, DGRO misses out on exposure to companies like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Netflix, Inc. (NFLX), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), just to name a few. As we can see below, DGRO has severely underperformed these standard index-tracking ETFs over the last decade due to the absence of these technology companies.

Dividend Growth

DGRO has not yet announced its first dividend for Q1 of 2026. Based on the last payout of $0.4470 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 2%. Although the starting yield may not be that exciting, I believe that the magic is in the fund's ability to provide an attractive rate of dividend growth over time. For instance, DGRO has been able to raise its dividends at a CAGR of 8.59% over the last ten year period. For better context, investors that started an initial position in DGRO exactly ten years ago would now have a yield on cost of ~5.5%.

The fund's strategy means that the process of seeing your annual dividend income increase year over year becomes effortless. This is great for investors that want to eventually create a tax-efficient dividend income stream that can be used as a supplemental income at some point in life. There are plenty of investors that would love to collect income from their portfolio without needing to sell shares and DGRO makes this possible.

To demonstrate the strength here, I ran a back test of an initial $10,000 investment at the start of 2017. This calculation assumes that no additional capital was invested after the initial $10 thousand. However, it does assume that all dividends were reinvested back into the accumulation of more shares. By simply remaining invested, DGRO investors would have seen their annual dividend income more than double over the years.

2017 Dividend Income: ~$246

2025 Dividend Income: ~$612

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that DGRO's portfolio strategy is well designed to navigate the current market conditions. If market uncertainty continues and the sell off of technology continues, DGRO is likely to remain more resilient than the tech-heavy indices. In a scenario where capital rotates out of the technology sector into more value-oriented stocks, DGRO is directly positioned to capitalize on this while providing attractive dividend growth over time. If markets trade sideways, DGRO can also outperform.

Even in a scenario where the pullback in technology is at its bottom, DGRO is also aligned to participate in the expansion of the technology market since it provides a meaningful allocation to high quality dividend-paying companies in the sector. So whatever ends up happening in the market, DGRO remains a great option for buy-and-hold investors that want to prioritize dividend growth.

data.title: SPYG: Low Fees, High Dependence On Mega-Caps
data.description:
The SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG), launched on 09/25/2000 by State Street Global Advisors, Inc. and managed by SSGA Funds Management, Inc., offers large-cap growth exposure in the U.S. equity market by tracking the S&P 500 Growth Index. It currently has ~$44 billion and charges an expense ratio of 0.04% per annum.

The ETF offers one of the cheapest ways to invest in large-cap growth stocks and has outperformed its pure-growth peer over time. Investors may want to understand the most recent performance challenges and the near-term risks before they rotate into a growth portfolio.

Methodology

The S&P 500 Growth Index selects the names within the S&P 500 (parent index) that have the strongest growth characteristics, based on revenue growth, earnings change to price ratio, and momentum.

It starts with the parent index and computes a growth and value score for each constituent. The growth score is calculated based on the following growth factors:

  • Three-year sales per share growth rate
  • Three-year net change in EPS over current price
  • 12-month price momentum.

The value factors are the following:

  • Book-to-price ratio
  • Earnings-to-price ratio
  • Sales-to-price ratio

Then, it sorts the style baskets using the Growth Rank / Value Rank ratio. The top names by this ratio cover ~33% of the parent index market capitalization and form the Growth basket. The bottom names (~33% of the parent index) form the Value basket. The middle is comprised of "blend" stocks.

What differentiates this methodology is that the index will include all the stocks from the growth basket, plus all of the blend basket stocks, but with a partial weight, depending on each stock's score distance from growth. The closer one blend stock is to growth and the farther from value, the more of its market cap is included in the index.

Portfolio

The top 10 holdings illustrate the aggressiveness of this ETF, as they represent almost 60% of the portfolio.

I'll have to say something about the top names, however, because the implications are more nuanced than you might guess. The market recently reacted very negatively to the latest earnings results of NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), initially shaving off as much as 5% of its market capitalization, despite continued growth and bullish guidance. This indicates that the market has been desensitized to the company's high growth trajectory. Over the course of seven months, shares have trended sideways. With over 14% of the portfolio, Nvidia could continue to be a drag on SPYG's overall performance.

Apple Inc. (AAPL), though a good long-term holding to have, hasn't been growing very fast lately. And Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) has been a considerable drag recently. Alphabet, Inc. (GOOG) has been growing faster, but most of that growth happened in the second half of 2025, when the market re-rated the stocks after certain idiosyncratic risks started appearing less threatening; the trend did not persist and it's slightly down YTD.

Although there is meaningful exposure to Financials, Health care, and Industrials, the sector breakdown shows a strong tech bias at nearly 50% of the fund. Given the present bearish sentiment towards software stocks, this implies a near-term headwind. The market has re-rated the industry after the risk that AI might cause a significant disruption was more appreciated.

Performance

A slightly cheaper alternative is the Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG), charging an expense ratio of 0.03%. It has a similar approach and, therefore, its top holdings and sector breakdown are almost the same.

For differentiated exposure to growth stocks within the S&P 500 index, the Invesco S&P 500 Pure Growth Index (RPG) is a decent alternative, but it charges an expense ratio of 0.35%. Unlike SPYG and VUG, it doesn't overlap growth stocks with blend stocks and the holdings are weighted by their growth scores, not their market caps.

Interestingly, in the long run, SPYG has significantly outperformed RPG, hinting at both fee and methodology efficiency. Additionally, its similar performance to VUG highlights the overlap of the portfolios, begging the question of why investors should choose the slightly more expensive SPYG.

I should also note, though, the lower annualized standard deviation that SPYG realized over the last ten years. Whether it's meaningfully lower to justify selecting it over VUG is up for debate, however.

Coming to more recent developments, after SPYG suffered through the brutal April 2025 selloff, it realized a rapid recovery and provided high returns until fall. After that recovery, though, its momentum declined as illustrated by the 50DMA (blue line) and it entered a consolidation phase, supported by the narrower Bollinger bands. In February, it consistently traded below the 50DMA and briefly touched its 200DMA on March 3.

February is a historically weak month for SPYG, as in the last decade it averaged a -0.1% return in that month.

However, the recent market sentiment shift that occurred serves as another explanation. With high CapEx guidance provided by hyperscalers and no near-term returns on those investments in AI infrastructure, the market has grown impatient. Coupled with the software stock selloff mentioned above, this has introduced a dark cloud over large-cap growth portfolios for the time being.

Risks

So the primary near-term risk that should be mentioned is the potential opportunity cost because the market is getting fearful. Until sentiment improves, growth funds and especially large-cap growth ETFs that have high Mag7 exposure could underperform more conservative ones like dividend or value-biased funds.

Over the long term, it's hard to build a case against SPYG or any similar large-cap growth ETF, for that matter. With that being said, there is always the risk that such funds become a drag on the performance of a diversified portfolio in case a regime that favors wider participation over concentrated tech exposure endures for years.

data.title: 'Software Is Dead, Long Live Software'
data.description:
The first two months of the year were a year to forget for the software sector. In just two months, the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) fell more than 22%, taking its total decline from its peak to over 30%.

In the early weeks of 2026, it seemed as though every weekend a new negative article about the sector was published, allowing nervous investors to worry all weekend about the death of software stocks at the hands of AI.

The most notable of these reports, so far, was the Citrini essay titled “The 2028 Global Intelligence Crisis.” Published on 2/22, it also coincided with a weekend blizzard in the Northeast.

When the markets opened for trading the following Monday on 2/23, the magnitude of the decline was likely exaggerated given the lower market liquidity.

Looking back at that report, though, in the short term at least, its publication appears to have been a clearing-out event for the market, as IGV has rallied 13.9% since the close on 2/23.

Over the course of that 13% rally, IGV has traded higher in eight of the last nine trading days, including today’s fractional gain as of midday. Not only has IGV traded consistently higher, but there has also been steady buying throughout the trading day.

In each of those nine days, even on the one day it traded lower, IGV traded higher from the open to close. While there have been five other streaks where IGV had more consecutive days of gains from the open to close, the current streak is tied with four other periods for the sixth-longest streak in the ETF's history. It’s always easier to see in hindsight, but underneath all the snow on 2/23, there was plenty of blood on the software streets.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Dangers Of The XLU 'Safety Trade'
data.description:
The Utilities sector has historically been a favorite for investors seeking consistent income and relative safety, especially in a downturn. Over the last few years, the sector has garnered additional interest insofar as it has been deemed to be a direct beneficiary of the AI infrastructure buildout we’ve seen during the same period, which is largely expected to continue going forward in an accelerated fashion.

In this article, I’m going to examine the investment merits of a popular way to gain exposure to this sector by looking at the State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU). I think there are a lot more risks here than many investors might realize, and, taken together, I think these risks merit a ‘sell’ rating for the ETF.

Background

To begin, it’s important to understand the state of play:

XLU saw a run-up culminating in mid-October of last year, coinciding with what looks, in retrospect, to have been peak euphoria in the AI trade. The rationale here, of course, was that Utilities are supposed to be a prime beneficiary of the AI infrastructure buildout, given the massive increases in demand for electricity that that buildout is expected to bring with it.

Most recently, over the last several weeks, there has been a run-up in XLU that has coincided with a general rotation to safety and perceived immunity from AI disruption. This has also seen areas like Consumer Staples and Consumer Defensives, as well as Industrials and Materials, outperform. And it has been against the backdrop of what I can only describe as confusion in the tech trade. Magnificent 7 stocks have underperformed as investors begin to question the financial viability of hyper-scaler capex, on the one hand, while, at the same time, numerous industries have sold off (most notably, SaaS companies) due to perceived existential threat from new AI tools, on the other hand.

Is This Sustainable?

I think investors who have enjoyed this most recent run-up, as well as the longer-term gains over the last few years in XLU, would be smart to question how long it can continue. In my opinion, there are a number of reasons to be pessimistic on this front.

High Valuations And High Expectations

To begin, it’s important to realize that XLU is currently trading at a relatively high valuation. The following chart shows the P/E ratio of the S&P 500 Utilities Sector, the benchmark of XLU:

As you can see, we are trading at historically very high levels for this sector, even when measured against the last decade (for anyone who wants to insist that nothing like what happened in 2002 or 2008 for this sector could happen again—not a course of action I would endorse). According to State Street’s webpage for XLU, the ETF is currently trading at a TTM P/E of 22.84 (forward P/E of 19.49). The point is that we are at elevated levels here, and elevated P/Es mean elevated expectations, which, in turn, mean elevated risk (in the case of failing to meet those expectations).

Additionally, according to industry data compiled by Aswath Damodaran, the Utilities industry is expected to grow at a rate of 6.91% over the next 5 years, implying a PEG ratio of 2.96. A PEG ratio this high suggests significant overvaluation, given the growth expectations. And this should be expected. Despite the talk that’s out there about how we’re going to see an absolute flurry of data centers being built out over the next 5 years, there are structural realities to how utility companies work, to how the building of power infrastructure works, and to how the permitting and connections process of new build-outs works, which mean that the lofty expectations currently being discussed in this area are likely to not be things that can actually be achieved.

The “Safety Trade” Label Might Not Hold Up

One prominent narrative that is out there regarding the Utilities sector is that it provides safety in a downturn. This narrative might not hold up this time around, however, if we face turbulent times ahead. Two main factors support this concern. Utilities go down in a downturn like everything else, even if not as much. High valuations can exacerbate the downside.

During the 2022 bear market, XLU outperformed the S&P 500, staying relatively flat for the year while the S&P 500 was down ~20%. However, during the last leg of the downturn (and the last legs of these downturns can oftentimes be the most violent) of that year, from mid-August to mid-October, XLU actually underperformed, losing ~21%, while the S&P 500 was down ~15%.

During the COVID crash, XLU was down ~32%, comparable to the S&P 500. And during the Great Financial Crisis, XLU was down ~48% from the beginning of 2008 to the lows in March of 2009, while the S&P 500 was down ~54% during the same period. The point is that history shows that XLU is not immune from going down in a downturn, just like everything else.

And, when you layer on top of this the issue that we’re currently trading at elevated valuations, that just adds fuel to the fire. A lot of times folks get into “safety” trades thinking that at least they won’t have to suffer as severe of a drawdown if a downturn occurs, all the while forgetting that the valuations that one pays matter. A particularly vivid example of this has to do with Coca-Cola (KO), another favorite “safety” trade. The company was trading for something like a 47x P/E in July of 1998. Investors thought there was no risk at all here. After all, this was Coca-Cola, a favorite of Warren Buffett. Nothing could go wrong. Meanwhile, investors who bought and held from that period went on to dramatically underperform the S&P 500 over the next several years. Valuations matter. Buying XLU at historically high valuations of the sort we’re currently witnessing only makes it more likely to underperform expectations in any future downturn.

The Risk Of Inflation Picking Back Up

Utilities stocks can oftentimes function as a substitute for long-term bonds in the minds of many investors, given the consistent cash flows they have historically provided. However, this means that they are susceptible to similar risks as well. Notably, in an environment of rising inflation, stocks of utilities companies should be expected to be devalued as their future cash flows would be worth less. Additionally, given the high capital intensity that characterizes the operations of companies within the Utilities space, they would seem to be particularly impacted by a highly inflationary environment. This is an issue Warren Buffett warned about in his famous 1977 Fortune article, How Inflation Swindles the Equity Investor. As the discussion in this article (which elaborates on the just-referenced Buffett article) highlights, the burden of high capital expenditures acts like a tapeworm, with ever-increasing costs of PP&E being particularly onerous to such firms in their efforts to generate meaningful value for shareholders.

And there are legitimate concerns in the current environment that suggest inflation is something investors should continue to be worried about. First, there is the fact that we have yet to hit the Fed’s inflation goal of 2% since the inflationary period of 2021-22:

Second, there is concern that the ongoing U.S.-Israeli-Iran conflict could spike oil prices for a prolonged period and potentially bring about stagflationary conditions reminiscent of periods from the 1970s.

These should be very real concerns for anyone considering XLU at today’s prices.

Potential Popping Of The AI Bubble

I have written extensively about the AI bubble, which you can read about here. I will not elaborate too much on those issues in this article, however, given that these other writings delve deeply into my views on this space. However, beyond what I highlighted earlier—namely, that the hype around data center build-out projections seems to far outstrip what is practically possible—I would simply underscore that, unless the industry comes up with a “killer app” soon (e.g., artificial general intelligence), it seems highly unlikely that the industry will be able to justify all the capital expenditures that have gone into this space over the last several years. When investors start to come to this conclusion (and I think they are already clearly starting to do so), there’s going to be a long way down for the AI trade and all the various industries and stocks that have seen their valuations bid up alongside it in the wake of all the hype of the past few years. Utilities stocks are likely to be included in this lot, for sure, in my view. As a result, I think investors in XLU should seriously evaluate the risks along this dimension.

Risks To The Thesis

So, how could I be wrong? Well, perhaps valuations aren’t terribly relevant at this point. Maybe we’ve moved into some “new economy” where such concerns are simply antiquated. Additionally, maybe the concerns I raise around the “safety trade” are not so compelling. Perhaps high valuations don’t exacerbate XLU’s standing as a “safety trade” for some reason or another. Furthermore, perhaps higher future inflation is not really a concern. Maybe we’ll luck out and avoid all the concerns folks have around inflation going forward. Finally, perhaps AI isn’t in a bubble. Maybe we don’t have significant risk in the stock market from such concerns, contrary to what I’ve suggested here and elsewhere.

Sure, these things are possible. As investors, though, I think we have to assess the degree to which they are probable. In my view, and as I’ve argued here, I think it’s far more likely that these risks to my thesis don’t come about than that they do, for all the reasons discussed. Time will tell, however. Nevertheless, investors should seriously attempt to assess where the probabilities lie on these issues, as they are about as germane to good decision-making in this regard as anything I can think of.

Investor Takeaway

Investors in XLU are taking on material risks at current prices and in the current investing environment. As I’ve argued, these revolve around valuation concerns, concerns around the extent to which XLU will act as a “safety” trade in a downturn, concerns around the possibility of higher future inflation, and concerns around the potential popping of the AI bubble. Taken together, these skew the risk-reward for XLU too much to the downside, in my view. For anyone seeking safety in a downturn and current income, I think short-term treasury bills (e.g., SGOV) offer a much more sensible alternative compared to something like XLU. However, regarding XLU, my recommendation is ‘sell.’

data.title: Rithm: This Time, I Agree With Wall Street
data.description:
RITM stock is loved by Wall Street

I last analyzed Rithm Capital (NYSE:RITM) stock on Jan 8, 2026. That article focused on the normalization of the yield curve and rated RITM as a buy. Since then, there have been a few new catalysts evolving around RITM stock. In the rest of this article, I will concentrate on the top 2 on my list: the updates the company provided in its FQ4 2025 earnings report (ER) and changes of its valuation multiples.

As a contrarian investor, I’ve written in the past arguing against the prevailing sentiment on RITM (and also other stocks). However, under current conditions, I found myself in agreement with ratings provided by Wall Street analysts. To wit, Wall Street analysts currently love RITM stock and issued a Strong Buy rating on average as you can see from the following screenshot. This collective rating came from a total 9 analysts in the last 90 days. More than half of them (5 out of 9) recommended the stock as a "Strong Buy" and the other 4 of them advised Buy. RITM stock currently also holds a bullish rating from Seeking Alpha (SA) authors, although the rating is slightly weaker at Buy.

With the above background, the rest of the article will A) explain why I agree with Wall Street’s bullish ratings, and B) identify the top financial trends that can support the bullish sentiment based on its FQ4 ER.

RITM stock’s transition into an asset manager

In a nutshell, my bullish stance on Rithm stems from the progress it has made to evolve from a traditional mortgage REIT into a diversified global alternative asset manager. I see several encouraging signs of the transition in the FQ4 2025 results. For readers unfamiliar with the background, the company has been working on the integration of various high-margin platforms like Sculptor Capital and Genesis Capital lately, with the goal to decouple its earnings potential from the volatile interest rate cycles that typically plague the mREIT sector. In FQ4, the company kept making progress on this front and acquired Crestline and Paramount Group (see CEO comments quoted below).

Yahoo! News: Michael Nierenberg, Chief Executive Officer of Rithm Capital, said, "2025 was a year of strategic progress for Rithm, marked by disciplined execution and consistent performance across every segment of our business. We delivered a 19% EAD return on equity for the full year and, through the targeted acquisitions of Crestline and Paramount Group, we continued to expand our diversified, alternative asset management platform.

With these integrations, the company now holds over $100 billion in investable assets. Such a position is quite potent and I see large odds for it to generate outsized returns through its newly created "unified ecosystem" rather than relying on the volatile spread. As a reflection, the company has delivered a strong FQ4 with double beats (see the next chart below). And note how sizable the bottom-line beat is in normalized terms. Its normalized EPS came in at $0.74 per share, vs. $0.60 of consensus estimate. The beat of $0.14 is thus more than 23% over consensus estimates.

RITM stock’s profitability and valuation

The potency of the integration is even more pronounced after I examined its profitability metrics. As summarized in the following ER slide, the company reported an 8% return on equity (ROE) for FY25 as of Q4. After reading the fine prints, I believe the headline ROE understates its true profitability (although an 8% ROE is quite robust in itself). This headline ROE is computed as the GAAP net interest return on equity divided by the average book value for the current year.

A better metric in my view should be based on the Earnings Available for Distribution (“EAD”), as EAD represents the actual amount of earnings that can be distributed to shareholders (but note that EAD is a non-GAAP measure). As such, the ROE based on EAD is calculated as the ratio between EAD and the average book value for the current year. Under this definition, the ROE based on EAD is 19% for FQ4, more than double the notional ROE. Also note that that the EAD ROE far exceeds the average dividend yield of 9.2% in the years, highlighting the sustainability of its payouts. Management also noted that FQ4 2025 marks the 25 consecutive quarters for which its EAD was greater than common dividends paid.

Despite the outsize EAD ROE, the stock now trades at a price that is significantly discounted from its book value. More specifically, the chart below illustrates the historical Price to Tangible Book Value (P/TBV) ratio for Rithm in the past 5 years from 2022 to the present. As seen, the stock currently trades at a P/TBV of only 0.756, which represents a discount of about 25% from the TBV. The current ratio is also noticeably below its historical average of 0.868.

Other risks and final thoughts

I won’t feel an mREIT analysis to be complete without taking a look at the stock’s dividend yield and also leverage ratios. The current and historical dividend yield for Rithm is shown below. As seen, RITM currently offers a double-digit yield of 10.45%, again, well within the 19% EAD ROE reported in its Q4 ER. Also note that the current yield sits well above the long-term average yield of 9.21%, which serves as another indicator of its attractive valuation in addition to the T/PBV discount as aforementioned.

In terms of leverage, the company’s balance sheet has become a bit more stretched with all the acquisition and integration efforts mentioned earlier. As a reflection, the chart below shows its debt-to-equity ratio on a quarterly basis for the past 5 years. As seen, the leverage ratio currently stands at 4.795x. As a reference point, the ratio has hovered around 4.4x between Q3 2024 and Q3 2025. The current leverage ratio is also slightly above the long-term average leverage of 4.717.

Finally, despite the progress the company has made to evolve its business model towards diversified asset management, a significant part of its current earnings streams still evolves around mortgage-related assets and thus inherently sensitive to interest rate fluctuations. A portfolio of particular interest involves its Mortgage Servicing Rights (MSR). Rithm has one of the largest portfolios of MSRs among major mREIT players. Its MSR portfolio can fluctuate as interest rates change and have caused substantially non-cash mark-to-market losses in the past, which in turn can cause GAAP earnings pressure and book value erosion.

To close, this reexamination on Rithm largely focused on the company’s FQ4 2025 ER, which reported continued evolution of its business model. With these updates, I found myself in agreement with Wall Street ratings this time as I see large odds for a successful transition of the company into a diversified asset manager. I expect this transition to lead to more consistent earnings and better profitability metrics. I begin to see signs of these benefits in its FQ4. Yet the market still values its book value with a large discount, thus creating a skewed return/risk ratio.

data.title: Employment Report: 92K Jobs Lost In February, Worse Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest employment report showed that 92,000 jobs were lost in February, down from January's 126,000 addition. This figure was worse than the projected addition of 58,000 jobs. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate inched up to 4.4%, coming in higher than the forecasted 4.3% rate.

Here is an excerpt from the Employment Situation Summary released this morning by the Bureau of Labor Statistics:

Total nonfarm payroll employment edged down by 92,000 in February, and the unemployment rate changed little at 4.4 percent, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Employment in health care decreased, reflecting strike activity. Employment in information and federal government continued to trend down.

Household Survey Data: Both the unemployment rate, at 4.4 percent, and the number of unemployed people, at 7.6 million, changed little in February.

Establishment Survey Data: Total nonfarm payroll employment edged down by 92,000 in February, following an increase in January (+126,000). Employment in health care decreased in February, reflecting strike activity. Employment in information and federal government continued to trend down. Payroll employment changed little on net in 2025.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in nonfarm employment over the last five years. The 3-month moving average is currently at 6,000.

For another view, here is the monthly percent change in nonfarm employment since 2000. We've added a 12-month moving average to highlight the long-term trend. The latest 12-month moving average is at 13,000.

Unemployment, Recessions, and Market Trends

The next chart illustrates the relationship between unemployment, recessions, and the S&P Composite since 1948. Unemployment is typically a lagging indicator that moves inversely to equity prices (the top series in the chart).

Notice the rising unemployment peaks in 1971, 1975, and 1982, which coincided with bear markets. A similar pattern briefly emerged during the COVID pandemic, but the impact was short-lived as irrational exuberance quickly took over. The latest unemployment rate stands at 4.441% (to three decimal places).

Now, let's examine the unemployment rate as a recession indicator - specifically, the cyclical troughs in the unemployment rate (UR). The next chart highlights a 12-month moving average of the UR, with its troughs marked.

Currently, the unemployment rate stands at 4.44%, higher than the latest 12-month moving average of 4.30%. As shown in the inset table, the correlation between these moving average troughs and the start of recessions is remarkably strong.

The most recent trough occurred 32 months ago, in June 2023, when the 12-month moving average of the unemployment rate fell to its lowest level since January 1970.

The next chart highlights the unemployment rate for those unemployed for 27 weeks or more. This rate has declined significantly from its all-time peak of 4.4% in April 2010. Following the COVID pandemic, it rose as high as 2.6% but quickly fell. This rate has recently been trending upwards and now stands at 1.1%.

How long does unemployment typically last? As the next chart shows, the latest data indicates that the average duration of unemployment is 25.7 weeks, the highest level in four years. Historically, this metric tends to rise during and after recessions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics’ broadest measure of unemployment, the U6 series, captures not only the unemployed but also the underemployed, marginally attached workers, and those who have stopped looking for work.

Often referred to as the 'real' unemployment rate, many economists consider it the most comprehensive gauge of labor market conditions. In contrast, the more widely cited unemployment rate only includes those who are unemployed and actively job-seeking within the past four weeks. The U6 series currently stands at 7.9%, down from 8.1% in January.

Labor Market Participation and Structural Shifts

For a broader perspective on the structural shifts in the U.S. labor market, the next chart illustrates the labor force participation rate. This metric measures the percentage of the civilian population aged 16 and over who are either employed or actively seeking work.

It provides a key estimate of an economy’s active workforce and serves as an important indicator of labor market health. Currently, the rate stands at 62.0%, its lowest level since the end of 2021.

Here’s another chart that provides a different perspective on the unemployment rate - unemployment divided by the labor force participation rate. This metric currently stands at 7.2%, up from 7.0% in January.

The next chart overlays the unemployment rate with the employment-population ratio, which measures the number of employed people as a percentage of the total civilian population aged 16 and over. The inverse correlation between the two series is clear.

The data also highlights the accelerating growth of women in the workforce and the rise of two-income households in the early 1980s. Before the COVID pandemic, the employment-population ratio fluctuated between 54.9% and 64.7%.

The lower end of this range dates back to the Eisenhower era - a time of predominantly one-income households, the Korean War, and the novelty of soft drinks being sold in cans. In April 2020, the global pandemic drove the employment-population ratio to a record low of 51.2%. The latest reading is at 59.3%, the lowest level in over four years.

Looking ahead, both the employment-population ratio and labor force participation rate will be key metrics to watch. The first wave of baby boomers continues to exert downward pressure on these figures. The oldest boomers became eligible for early retirement at the onset of the Great Recession, and their transition to full retirement age will continue through 2030.

Notes: The start date of 1948 in the charts above was determined by the earliest monthly employment data collected by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The best source for the historical data is the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

The S&P Composite is a splice of the S&P 500, which started in 1957, with the S&P 90, which preceded it. ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Capital One: Attractive Despite Growing Credit Risks
data.description:
Shares of Capital One Financial Corporation (COF) have been a modest performer over the past year, gaining about 6%. However, recent performance has been worse, with shares steadily dropping over $60 so far in 2026. There are growing concerns about the economic outlook, with job growth weak and the Iran conflict sparking a large rise in oil prices. This type of price shock could stress consumers already facing a difficult affordability environment, and with its focus on credit card lending, Capital One is very exposed to consumer credit trends. I last covered COF in October, upgrading shares to a “Buy,” which was a very poor call, and the stock is down 17% since then. With such a changed macro picture, now is a good time to revisit shares and determine next steps.

We have seen crude oil prices rise significantly over the past few months, rallying from the $50s toward $90. This is a material price shock, and while it will take some time to filter through to gas prices, it could potentially lift headline inflation by ~0.3%-0.5%. Because oil is a key cost for transportation, it can have a knock-on effect on many good prices beyond consumers’ spending at the pump. All else equal, this shock will reduce consumers’ discretionary spending power and weaken the economy. A massive uncertainty is whether this conflict ends shortly or spirals further, which, frankly, is unknowable. While I believe the economy can withstand $80-$100 oil, a move past $100 makes recession a greater risk.

Ultimately, there are multiple causes of default cycles in consumer credit, but oil has historically played a role. Before the financial crisis, oil prices approached $150, and gasoline jumped from $2 to $4 in a year. That coincided with a large rise in delinquencies. From 2014 to 2019, gasoline prices were fairly low, and consumer credit performance was consistently strong. Post-COVID, we saw oil prices (alongside many other prices) spike, and consumer credit worsened from unsustainably low levels.

I am not arguing that oil prices single-handedly drive delinquencies or the economy, but they play a role. Employment is also an important driver of delinquencies. The rise in delinquencies in 1996, 2001, and 2008 all coincided with a drop in employment (note employment growth is inverted here), and when employment improves, delinquencies fall. This makes sense, as consumers are more likely to struggle to pay bills when they lose their income (I excluded the COVID pandemic from this chart as massive stimulus insulated consumer incomes from a temporary rise in unemployment). This is what makes the deterioration of the employment situation, with 92,000 jobs lost in February, so concerning.

All else equal, when oil prices rise when employment falls, it creates a difficult environment for consumer credit. Well, both of these may now be occurring. For now, I believe investors should not yet be too fearful of labor market dynamics. A significant drop in immigration has reduced potential employment growth, driving much of the recent deceleration, and a strike also made February’s report unusually weak. In my view, the labor market is flashing “orange” warning signs but is not yet flashing “red.” While my personal view is that a ground invasion of Iran is highly unlikely, risks to oil prices are also increasing. Combined, this has led to a much worse backdrop for consumer credit, and investors should not be blind to these risks. I now expect delinquencies to rise modestly in 2026, up from my prior view of a flattish performance.

These fears have built as the company has reported mixed results. In the fourth quarter, Capital One earned $3.86 per share, which missed expectations by $0.28, a step back after a stellar Q3. During Q4, Capital One built $302 million of allowances as charge-offs increased $360 million sequentially. This build reversed some of the strength that made me enthusiastic post-Q3 results. This larger-than-expected reserve build drove the earnings miss. COF ended the quarter with $23.4 billion of allowances, or 5.16% of loans. Still, it is worth keeping in mind that reserves are lower than when COF completed the Discover acquisition. In other words, there is not a sign this deal is progressing worse than modeled.

Net charge-offs were 4.91% in its credit card unit, up 30 bps sequentially but down from 6% last year. 30+ day delinquencies across the firm were 3.59%, up 9 bps sequentially but down 39 bps from last year. Typically, we see delinquencies rise in Q4 and then fall in Q1 due to tax refunds. I expect this to still occur in Q1, as the oil shock will be felt more clearly in 2026. While the Q4 charge-off increase was a bit more than expected and COF took a cautious stance on allowances, delinquency trends are not especially troubling.

Capital One’s appetite for acquisitions shows no signs of slowing down. After closing its Discover deal last year, it now plans to buy Brex for $5.15 billion via a 50/50 combination of cash and stock. While COF has a premier consumer credit card franchise, its business offerings have lagged competitors like JPMorgan (JPM) and American Express (AXP). Brex should help COF gain a better foothold in this segment, especially among early-stage companies. While strategically sound, I do not expect Brex to materially contribute to financial results before 2028.

Separately during Q4, COF completed the sale of an $8.8 billion home loan portfolio inherited from Discover. This sale generated a $483 million profit and will allow COF to dedicate capital to higher-margin, core businesses. The Discover deal continues to progress as planned, though I expect operating expenses to be a bit higher as COF pushes into higher-end credit cards, which tend to require greater marketing and loyalty rewards spending.

Importantly, Capital One maintains an excellent balance sheet with a 14.3% tier 1 common equity capital ratio (CET1); this is especially important amid elevated macro uncertainty. This is well above the 11-11.5% that COF needs. The Brex purchase will reduce capital by 40 bps. I do not expect this to reduce buybacks in the near term given its excess capital position. COF did $2.5 billion of Q4 buybacks, and I expect about $8 billion of 2026 repurchases. This should reduce its share count by about 6%. Plus, shares offer a 1.7% dividend yield, for an all-in capital return yield of about 8%.

Back in October, I saw Capital One earning $19.50-$21 this year. As we learned last year during the Liberation Day sell-off, it can be important not to overreact to near-term actions from an Administration that can quickly adapt and shift strategies. That said, there is greater uncertainty in this situation, as wars can have unpredictable outcomes and reactions. However, there are things the Administration can also do to truncate downside economic risks, like SPR releases, easing sanctions, etc. On balance, I think it is prudent to assume modestly more reserve builds than previously.

As such, I am now looking for $18.75-$20.50 in EPS. That leaves shares just under 10x earnings with an 8% capital return yield. I view this as an attractive level for a company with no material private credit exposure and a history of solid credit card underwriting, even recognizing there is more risk to consumer credit trends. As such, I reiterate my "Buy" rating, and as investors look for opportunities in the sell-off, I see COF as a name poised to outperform. I believe shares should at least recover to the $210-$220 area, or 11x earnings, aided by support from a strong buyback program.
